title: Shoulder Injuries: What to Watch For
summary: Learn to spot the warning signs of a serious shoulder injury after a fall or collision, and what to do right away.

sections.heading: Why this matters
body: The shoulder can get hurt in falls, collisions, and tackles, and also from doing the same overhead motion — like throwing or serving — over and over.
You don't need to know exactly what's wrong inside the shoulder. Your job is to notice that something looks or feels wrong, and get a trusted adult right away.

sections.heading: How shoulders get hurt
body: Most shoulder injuries at your age happen one of two ways.
list: A fall or hit — landing hard on an arm or shoulder, getting tackled, or crashing during a bike ride or on the field can jar or injure the shoulder.
Overuse — throwing, serving, or swimming with the same arm motion thousands of times can make the shoulder sore and achy over weeks, even without a single fall or hit.

sections.heading: Signs something's wrong
body: After a fall, hit, or hard landing, watch for these signs that the shoulder is seriously hurt, not just bumped:
list: The arm is held away from the body in an odd position, and the athlete supports it with the other hand
There's an obvious bump, dent, or the shoulder looks a different shape than the other side
The athlete can't move the arm normally, or refuses to move it at all
Pain is severe, not just a little sore
Numbness or tingling in the arm or hand

sections.heading: What to do right away
body: If you see any of these signs, follow these steps:
list: Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or other trusted adult immediately.
Support the arm gently in whatever position it's already in — don't try to move it, straighten it, or push it back into place.
Let the adult decide on ice, a sling, or calling for more help.

callout.title: Never try to put a shoulder 'back in place'
callout.text: Sometimes a teammate or coach might say they can pop a shoulder back into place. Never let anyone try this, and never try it yourself. Moving an injured shoulder the wrong way can cause serious damage. Support the arm as it is, and get an adult right away.

sections.heading: Achy shoulders from overuse
body: Not every shoulder problem comes from a big hit. Athletes who throw, serve, or swim a lot can get a shoulder that aches, feels weak, or hurts when reaching overhead — even without ever falling or getting hit.
This kind of pain builds up slowly over practices and games instead of happening all at once. It still matters just as much as a sudden injury.

callout.title: Don't push through shoulder pain
callout.text: If your shoulder aches during or after overhead activity like throwing, serving, or swimming, tell a coach or trusted adult instead of playing through it. Pain that keeps coming back is your body's way of asking for a break and a check-up.

sections.heading: Getting back to sport
body: Deciding when a shoulder injury is healed enough to play again is not something a student athlete decides on their own — that call belongs to an athletic trainer or a doctor.
Going back to throwing, hitting, or contact sports too soon, before the shoulder has had time to heal, can turn a small problem into a much bigger one.

quiz.question: Which of these is a sign that a shoulder injury might be serious?
choices: The athlete holds the arm away from the body and won't move it
The athlete says the shoulder feels fine
There's no pain at all
The athlete can raise both arms normally
quiz.explanation: Holding the arm away from the body and refusing to move it is a warning sign of a serious shoulder injury. Normal movement and no pain are signs that the shoulder is probably fine.

quiz.question: A teammate crashes during a bike ride and lands on his shoulder. He's holding his arm oddly against his body and there's a visible bump at the top of his shoulder. What should you do first?
choices: Try to push the bump back into place
Stop, tell a trusted adult right away, and support the arm as it is
Tell him to shake it off and keep riding
Have him raise the arm overhead to test if it still works
quiz.explanation: The first step is always to stop, get a trusted adult, and support the arm without moving it. Pushing on the bump or testing the arm's motion could make the injury worse.

quiz.question: What are the two main ways shoulders usually get hurt for athletes your age?
choices: Only from lifting heavy weights
Only from cold weather
Falls/hits and overuse from repeated overhead motion
Shoulders can't really get injured until adulthood
quiz.explanation: Most shoulder injuries come from a fall or collision, or from doing the same overhead motion (like throwing or serving) over and over. Weightlifting and cold weather aren't the typical causes at this age.

quiz.question: A swimmer's shoulder has been aching for two weeks, especially when she reaches overhead, but she never fell or got hit. Does this still matter?
choices: No, it only counts as an injury if something dramatic happened
No, achy shoulders always go away on their own without telling anyone
Yes, but only if the pain becomes unbearable
Yes, she should tell a coach or trusted adult even though there was no single fall or hit
quiz.explanation: Shoulder pain that builds up slowly from overuse matters just as much as a sudden injury. She should tell a coach or trusted adult, not wait for the pain to become severe or hope it fixes itself.

quiz.question: If someone offers to pop an injured shoulder back into place on the field, what should happen?
choices: Never let anyone attempt this — support the arm and get an adult
Let them try it if they say they've done it before
Have the injured athlete try to pop it back in themselves
Wait until after the game to worry about it
quiz.explanation: No one should ever try to move an injured shoulder back into place on the field, no matter their experience. The right move is to support the arm as found and get a trusted adult immediately.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT something you should do for a suspected shoulder injury?
choices: Tell a trusted adult right away
Straighten or reposition the arm to make it look normal
Support the arm gently in the position it's already in
Stop playing immediately
quiz.explanation: You should never try to straighten or reposition an injured arm. Stopping play, telling an adult, and supporting the arm as-is are the correct first steps.

quiz.question: Numbness or tingling in the arm or hand after a shoulder injury means:
choices: It's nothing to worry about
The athlete should keep playing to see if it goes away
Only ice is needed, no adult required
This is a warning sign that needs a trusted adult right away
quiz.explanation: Numbness or tingling is a warning sign that something serious may be going on and needs adult attention right away, not something to wait out or manage alone.

quiz.question: Who should decide when an athlete's shoulder injury has healed enough to return to play?
choices: The athlete, based on how it feels that day
A teammate who has had a shoulder injury before
An athletic trainer or doctor
Whichever adult is standing closest
quiz.explanation: Return-to-play decisions after a shoulder injury should come from an athletic trainer or doctor, not the athlete's own judgment or a teammate's opinion.

flashcards.front: Shoulder injury warning sign: arm position
flashcards.back: The arm is held away from the body in an odd position, often supported by the other hand.
An obvious bump, dent, or the shoulder looking a different shape compared to the other side.
The athlete can't move the arm normally, or refuses to move it at all.
Numbness or tingling in the arm or hand — always tell a trusted adult right away.

flashcards.front: Two main causes of shoulder injuries
flashcards.back: A fall or hit (like a tackle or crash), or overuse from repeating the same overhead motion many times.

flashcards.front: First step after a suspected shoulder injury
Second step after a suspected shoulder injury
flashcards.back: Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or other trusted adult immediately.

flashcards.front: How to support an injured arm
flashcards.back: Support it gently in the position it's already in. Never try to move, straighten, or reposition it.

flashcards.front: Never do this for a shoulder injury
flashcards.back: Never try to pop or push a shoulder back into place, and never let anyone else try it either.
Aching or weakness that builds up over weeks from repeated throwing, serving, or swimming — no single fall or hit needed.
Repeated overhead pain is a sign to tell an adult and take a break, not to keep playing.
An athletic trainer or doctor decides when a shoulder injury has healed enough to play again, not the athlete or a teammate.
